coredata.dc:title: Dual-N co-functionalized carbon dots for electrochemiluminescence sensing of perfluorooctanoic acid
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Hongyan
Zhao, Jinwen
Chen, Shihong
Tan, Kejun
coredata.dc:description: Perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) has been identified as a toxic pollutant. Due to its persistence and bioaccumulation in the environment, even at concentrations far below the limit of detection (LOD) of existing analytical methods, it poses a significant threat to health and the ecosystem. It is urgent to develop a highly sensitive strategy for PFOA analysis. This work explored aptamer (Apt) recognition-driven toehold-mediated strand displacement reaction (TSDR) as an efficient signal amplification strategy for PFOA detection. Tartaric acid, urea and ammonia water were used to prepare carbon dots (CDs). As N sources, urea primarily regulated the electronic structure, while ammonia water mainly passivated the surface defects of CDs to inhibit non-radiative transitions. The resulting dual-N co-functionalized CDs (bi-N-CDs) exhibited strong electrochemiluminescence (ECL) emission at −1.7 V. The complexes formed by bi-N-CDs and gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) were modified on the electrode for capturing hairpin H1, obtaining the aptasensor. Subsequently, an Apt recognition-driven TSDR was designed to convert trace amounts of PFOA into a large amount of output DNA (S1 and S2), which hybridized with initiator S4 to prevent quencher ferrocene (Fc)-labeled H2 from being introduced onto the aptasensor, resulting in a signal-on state. In the absence of PFOA, S4 triggered the catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA), thereby enabling the Fc-H2 to be assembled onto the aptasensor, resulting in a signal-off state. As a result, ultrasensitive detection of PFOA was achieved with a LOD of 42.7 fM. The integration of bi-N-CDs with TSDR established a novel and ultrasensitive ECL platform for PFOA analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Perfluorooctanoic acid
Dual-N co-functionalized carbon dots
Aptasensor
Nucleic acid amplification strategy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of output DNA (S1 and S2)
Construction and measurement of ECL aptasensor
Morphological characterization of bi-N-CDs and Au NPs
Response performance of aptasensor to PFOA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images and corresponding size distribution histograms for bi-N-CDs (A, B) and au NPs (C, D).
XPS spectra of the full region of (A) bi-N-CDs and corresponding element: (B) C1s, (C) N1s, and (D) O1s.

(A) FT-IR spectra of CDs (black line) and bi-N-CDs (red line). (B) PL and UV−vis absorption spectra of bi-N-CDs aqueous solution. (C) 3D ECL spectrum at bi-N-CDs/GCE in 0.10 M PBS with 10 mM S2O8 2-. (D) PL spectra of bi-N-CDs aqueous solution and ECL spectrum of bi-N-CDs/GCE in 0.10 M PBS with 10 mM S2O8 2-.

(A) Effect of the excitation voltage window on ECL response of bi-N-CDs/GCE (a-I: 0 ∼ −1.2 V, 0 ∼ −1.3 V, 0 ∼ −1.4 V, 0 ∼ −1.5 V, 0 ∼ −1.6 V, 0 ∼ −1.7 V, 0 ∼ −1.8 V, 0 ∼ −1.9 V, 0 ∼ −2.0 V). (B) ECL responses of (a) bare GCE in 0.10 M PBS with 10 mM S2O8 2-, bi-N-CDs/GCE in 0.10 M PBS (b) without and (c) with 10 mM S2O8 2-, (d) bi-N-CDs/GCE in 0.10 M deoxygenated PBS with 10 mM S2O8 2-. DPV curves of bi-N-CDs/GCE in PBS (C) without and (D) with 10 mM S2O8 2- in the (a) presence and (b) absence of dissolved oxygen (insert: zoomed-in view of the area within the dashed box). (E) ECL responses of (a) CDs/GCE, (b) urea-CDs/GCE, (c) NH3-CDs/GCE, (d) bi-N-CDs/GCE in PBS with 10 mM S2O8 2-. (F) DPV curves of (a) CDs/GCE, (b) urea-CDs/GCE, (c) NH3-CDs/GCE, (d) bi-N-CDs/GCE in PBS without 10 mM S2O8 2- (insert: zoomed-in view of the area within the dashed box). DPV measurement: working voltage of 0 ∼ −1.7 V with an amplitude of 50 mV, pulse width of 0.05 s and pulse period of 0.5 s. ECL measurement: scanning potential of 0 ∼ −1.7 V, scanning rate of 300 mV/s, amplifier series of 4 and PMT voltage of 800 V.

(A) CV profiles and (B) EIS plots of each step electrodes in [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution (5.0 mM) (insert of B: equivalent circuit): (a) bare GCE, (b) au NPs@bi-N-CDs/GCE, (c) H1/Au NPs@bi-N-CDs/GCE, and (d) HT/H1/Au NPs@bi-N-CDs/GCE. (C) ECL responses of (a) aptasensor, aptasensor incubated with the mixture of (b) Fc-H2 and S4, (c) Fc-H2, S4 and output DNA (S1 and S2) in 0.10 M PBS with 10 mM S2O8 2-. scanning rate of 0.10 V/s and working voltage of −0.20 ∼ + 0.60 V for CV measurement. Eis measurement: frequency of 10−1 ∼105 Hz and working voltage of 0.22 V with an amplitude of 5 mV. ECL measurement: scanning rate of 300 mV/s, amplifier series of 4 and PMT voltage of 800 V.

(A) ECL response of the aptasensor to PFOA (a-h: 0.10 pM, 1.0 pM, 10 pM, 0.10 nM, 1.0 nM, 10 nM, 0.10 μM, 1.0 μM). (B) calibration curve of PFOA detection. (C) Stability of the aptasensor under continuous scanning for 10 cycles. (D) Selectivity of the aptasensor to blank sample, 10 μM of interfering substance (PFB, PFBS, PFHxA, PFPeA) alone, 100 nM PFOA and their mixture. (E) Reproducibility of the aptasensor with 0.10 nM PFOA. (F) Storage stability of the aptasensor with 0.10 μM PFOA. Ecl measurement: scanning rate of 300 mV/s, amplifier series of 4 and PMT voltage of 800 V. error bars: standard deviations of three measurements.
(A) Synthesis of au NPs@bi-N-CDs. (B) fabrication process of the ECL aptasensor. (C) Apt recognition-driven TSDR amplification.
Detection results of PFOA in Jialing river samples by UPLC-MS/MS and ECL methods.

head.ce:title: Dual-N co-functionalized carbon dots for electrochemiluminescence sensing of perfluorooctanoic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) has been identified as a toxic pollutant. Due to its persistence and bioaccumulation in the environment, even at concentrations far below the limit of detection (LOD) of existing analytical methods, it poses a significant threat to health and the ecosystem. It is urgent to develop a highly sensitive strategy for PFOA analysis. This work explored aptamer (Apt) recognition-driven toehold-mediated strand displacement reaction (TSDR) as an efficient signal amplification strategy for PFOA detection. Tartaric acid, urea and ammonia water were used to prepare carbon dots (CDs). As N sources, urea primarily regulated the electronic structure, while ammonia water mainly passivated the surface defects of CDs to inhibit non-radiative transitions. The resulting dual-N co-functionalized CDs (bi-N-CDs) exhibited strong electrochemiluminescence (ECL) emission at −1.7 V. The complexes formed by bi-N-CDs and gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) were modified on the electrode for capturing hairpin H1, obtaining the aptasensor. Subsequently, an Apt recognition-driven TSDR was designed to convert trace amounts of PFOA into a large amount of output DNA (S1 and S2), which hybridized with initiator S4 to prevent quencher ferrocene (Fc)-labeled H2 from being introduced onto the aptasensor, resulting in a signal-on state. In the absence of PFOA, S4 triggered the catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA), thereby enabling the Fc-H2 to be assembled onto the aptasensor, resulting in a signal-off state. As a result, ultrasensitive detection of PFOA was achieved with a LOD of 42.7 fM. The integration of bi-N-CDs with TSDR established a novel and ultrasensitive ECL platform for PFOA analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) technology has garnered significant attention due to its precise control and high sensitivity, demonstrating enormous application potential in fields such as biomedicine [1], environmental monitoring [2], food safety [3], drug development [4], and disease diagnosis [5]. To realize the high-sensitivity advantage of ECL detection, luminophores are of paramount importance [6]. Among them, carbon dots (CDs) have received considerable attention due to their facile preparation, good water solubility, and high stability [7]. However, their in-depth application in the ECL field is limited by their low ECL efficiency. Doping modification is a common method to enhance ECL efficiency of CDs. In particular, N sources are doped to transform the electronic structure of CDs, enabling the formed N-CDs to exhibit improved ECL efficiency [8]. Since single-atom doping produces limited active sites in N-CDs and thus limited efficiency improvement of N-CDs, more attention has been paid to enhancing the efficiency of CDs by multi-atom co-doping [9]. For example, Liu et al. co-doped Se and N into CDs, using Se source with excellent redox activity to further increase the active sites, thereby improving ECL efficiency of SeN-CDs [10]. Yang et al. co-doped P and N into CDs, using P source with high electron-donating ability to further improve ECL efficiency of PN-CDs [11]. However, these multi-atom co-doping strategies commonly introduce high-density structural defects on the surface of CDs, which serve as non-radiative recombination centers and still cause insufficient ECL efficiency [12]. Recent studies have shown that ammonia water can act as a passivation agent, effectively reducing surface defect states of CDs, suppressing non-radiative transitions, and enhancing the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs [13]. The unique properties of ammonia water as a passivation agent augur great potential as N source doping to improve ECL performance of CDs, which has not been previously reported.

Nucleic acid amplification technology is of vital importance for enhancing analytical performance [14,15]. Among various nucleic acid amplification strategies, as an enzyme-free isothermal nucleic acid amplification technique, toehold-mediated strand displacement reaction (TSDR) has attracted significant attention. The TSDR mechanism involves toehold-mediated binding of a target strand to a preassembled three-stranded DNA complex which contains trigger, blocker, and reporter strands. Subsequent branch migration of the fuel strand competitively releases the target strand from the complex, enabling the cyclic utilization of the target strand and signal amplification [16]. The high controllability, efficient amplification capability, flexible design and self-catalytic (no additional enzyme or energy input required) characteristics of TSDR make it demonstrate significant advantages in ultra-sensitive biosensing for microRNAs, proteins, and small molecules analysis [17]. For example, Wang et al. constructed a self-powered sensor based on an enzymatic biofuel cell through a two-step TSDR reaction, enabling efficient microRNA detection [18]. Liu et al. coupled a target-triggered bipedal DNA walker with TSDR for microRNA detection [19]. However, in these conventional TSDR systems, only one of the two output strands is typically used in subsequent reactions, resulting in low molecular utilization efficiency. To address this limitation, an improved TSDR with two output strands for maximal signal generation represents a promising strategy for enhancing biosensor performance.

Perfluorocarbons (PFCs) are a class of fluorinated hydrocarbon compounds synthesized in the late 20th century [20]. Due to exceptional chemical stability, fire resistance, weather resistance, and surface activity [21], they have found widespread applications in agriculture, industry, food packaging, and firefighting [22]. Among them, perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) is one of the most widely used PFCs. The robustness of the C-F bond makes PFOA difficult to degrade, thus accumulating in soil, water bodies and even drinking water [23]. PFOA has been identified as a toxic environmental pollutant, and exposure to it is associated with elevated cholesterol levels and increased risks of major diseases including bladder and testicular cancer [24]. Furthermore, the presence of PFOA has also been found in neonatal cord blood, and prolonged exposure to PFOA can severely impair the infant's thyroid hormone balance, eventually leading to cognitive deficits and impaired brain function [25]. Globally, the control of PFOA is becoming increasingly stringent. China also strictly limits its production and use, with the national drinking water standard GB 5749–2022 stipulating a maximum limit of 80 ng/L for PFOA [26]. Furthermore, the persistence and bioaccumulation of PFOA in the environment mean that even at concentrations far below the limit can pose a threat to human health and the ecological environment. Therefore, achieving highly sensitive detection of PFOA remains a critical issue that urgently needs to be addressed.

The primary method for PFOA detection is chromatography-mass spectrometry (CMS) [27]. While providing reliable results and limit of detection (LOD) at ng/L level, it has complex instrument operation and high analytical costs [28]. Electrochemistry [29] and Raman spectroscopy [30] have also been applied for PFOA detection. They are simpler than CMS, but the LOD can only reach the ng/L level. In fact, LOD at ng/L level is not sufficient for PFOA detection due to its persistence and bioaccumulation in the environment. Although highly sensitive and controllable ECL technology has shown great potential in the detection of environmental pollutants, its application in the detection of PFOA is still in the preliminary stage. Chen et al. initially explored molecularly imprinted polymer-modified g-C3N4 as ECL luminophore for PFOA detection [31]. However, its sensitivity was still inadequate due to insufficient specificity of the imprinted binding sites. To address the challenge of trace PFOA detection, our research team exploited an ECL aptasensor with aptamer (Apt)-driven nucleic acid amplification for PFOA detection and the LOD was as low as 1.97 fM [32]. This demonstrated that the combination of a nucleic acid amplification strategy with ECL technology was an effective approach to improve the detection sensitivity of PFOA.

In view of the research status of CDs as ECL emitters and ECL detection of PFOA, this work developed an ECL aptasensor for ultra-sensitive detection of PFOA using dual-N co-functionalized carbon dots (bi-N-CDs) as ECL emitters and Apt recognition-driven TSDR as efficient signal amplification strategy. As depicted in Scheme 1A, bi-N-CDs were prepared using tartaric acid, urea and ammonia water. Here, urea primarily regulated the electronic structure of CDs, and ammonia water mainly passivated CDs surface to reduce surface defect states and decrease non-radiative transitions, so bi-N-CDs exhibited strong ECL emission at −1.7 V with K2S2O8 as coreactant. The complexes of bi-N-CDs, gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) and chitosan were prepared and further modified on the surface of glassy carbon electrode (GCE). Subsequently, sulfhydryl group-labeled hairpin H1 was assembled on GCE via Au-S bonding, and then the nonspecific sites were blocked with hexanethiol (HT). Scheme 1B depicts the assembly of aptasensor and its ECL response to PFOA. When PFOA was absent, S4 acted as an initiator to trigger catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA) process, allowing the quencher ferrocene (Fc)-labeled H2 to be introduced on GCE, quenching the ECL signal. When PFOA was present, it could specifically bind to the Apt in double-stranded DNA (L0-Apt) anchored on magnetic beads (MBs) to release L0. The liberated L0 then hybridized with L1 in three-stranded DNA (L1-S1-S2) immobilized on MBs, outputting S2 and exposing the chain replacement fulcrum in L1 so that L1 hybridized with S3 to release L0 and output DNA S1. L0 could continue to react with the next S1-S2-L1. As a result, a large amount of S1 and S2 were output through TSDR (Scheme 1C). With the introduction of the released S1 and S2 on the aptasensor, they hybridized with initiator S4 to prevent Fc-H2 be introduced on the aptasensor, resulting in a signal-on state and enabling the quantitative analysis of PFOA. This work innovatively utilized passivation reagent ammonia water to inhibit the non-radiative transitions within CDs, thus significantly improving ECL emission from bi-N-CDs. Furthermore, the designed TSDR can fully utilize the two output chains to participate in the subsequent reaction, significantly improving molecular utilization efficiency and detection sensitivity. The integration of bi-N-CDs with Apt recognition-driven TSDR established an ultrasensitive ECL system for PFOA detection.
Supplementary Material presents the information about all instruments, reagents and materials, and the sequences of oligonucleotides (Table S1).

A simple one-step solvothermal method was used to synthesize bi-N-CDs. First, L-tartaric acid (0.15 g) and urea (0.18 g) were dissolved in 3.0 mL of N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) under ultrasonication for 5.0 min at room temperature to form a homogeneous solution. After 2.0 mL of ammonia water (25 % ∼ 28 % mass fraction) was added, the mixture solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5.0 h. Subsequently, the reaction products were dialyzed using a dialysis membrane (1k Da molecular weight cut-off) for 8.0 h at room temperature to remove impurities, and then subjected to vacuum rotary evaporation at 80 °C for 5.0 h to obtain the final yellowish-brown bi-N-CDs, which were dissolved in ultrapure water to prepare bi-N-CDs aqueous solution (1.0 mg/mL) for future use.
As control samples, CDs without N doping, urea doped CDs (urea-CDs), ammonia water doped CDs (NH3-CDs) were prepared using the similar preparation methods for bi-N-CDs.

First, Au NPs stock solution was prepared and the detailed synthesis procedure is described in the Supplementary Material. Subsequently, 0.30 mL of the prepared Au NPs was mixed with 1.0 mL bi-N-CDs aqueous solution (1.0 mg/mL) and 0.30 mL 0.5 % (w/v) chitosan acetic acid solution, and stirred for 10 min to yield Au NPs@bi-N-CDs solution, which was then stored at 4.0 °C for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of output DNA (S1 and S2)
ce:section.ce:para: Amino-labeled L0 (12 μM, 25 μL) reacted with Apt (12 μM, 25 μL) at 37 °C for 2.0 h to produce L0-Apt. Subsequently, 15 μL of amino-labeled L1 (18 μM), 15 μL of S1 (18 μM) and 15 μL of S2 (18 μM) were hybridized at 37 °C for 2.0 h to form L1-S1-S2. Afterwards, 20 μL of carboxy group-labeled MBs were activated using N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 0.10 mM) and N-(3-(dimethylamino) propyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 0.40 mM) for 15 min. Then, 10 μL of L0-Apt (6.0 μM) and 30 μL of L1-S1-S2 (6.0 μM) were incubated with the activated MBs at 4.0 °C for 14 h to assemble L0-Apt and L1-S1-S2 on MBs. After the resulting MBs were magnetically separated, they were mixed with 10 μL of PFOA at different concentrations and 40 μL of 12.5 mM Mg2 + Tris-EDTA (TE) buffer and reacted at 37 °C for 2.0 h, followed by removal of the supernatant using magnetic separation. Later, 50 μL of S3 (3.0 μM) was introduced and reacted at 37 °C for 2.0 h to initiate the TSDR process, outputting S1 and S2. Finally, magnetic separation was carried out to collect the supernatant containing S1 and S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and measurement of ECL aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: The assembly of ECL aptasensor is depicted in Scheme 1B. Firstly, bare GCE (Φ = 4.0 mm) was pretreated by the method outlined in the reference [33]. Then, 15 μL of Au NPs@bi-N-CDs solution was dropped on GCE surface and dried at 37 °C for 2.0 h, followed by the incubation of sulfhydryl group-labeled hairpin H1 (1.0 μM, 8.0 μL) on Au NPs@bi-N-CDs/GCE (4.0 °C, 12 h) to assemble H1 on GCE by Au-S bonding. The nonspecific sites on GCE were blocked using HT (1.0 mM, 10 μL) at 4.0 °C for 1.0 h to obtain the aptasensor (HT/H1/Au NPs@bi-N-CDs/GCE). Subsequently, the mixture of S4 and Fc-H2 (10 μL and 1.0 μM for each) was incubated on the aptasensor so that CHA process occurred (37 °C, 2.0 h) and Fc-H2 was assembled on the aptasensor for quenching ECL emission. When the aptasensor was incubated with the mixture containing output S1 and S2 (3.0 μL), S4 (1.0 μM, 3.0 μL) and Fc-H2 (1.0 μM, 3.0 μL) at 37 °C for 2.0 h, S1 and S2 hybridized with S4 to prevent Fc-H2 be introduced on the aptasensor. The output S1 and S2 increased with the augment of PFOA, which bound with more S4, and Fc-H2 introduced on the aptasensor decreased, so ECL emission increased accordingly, realizing the ultrasensitive detection of PFOA.

ECL signals were detected in phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4, 0.10 M) with 10 mM K2S2O8. The scanning potential of 0 ∼ −1.7 V, scanning rate of 300 mV/s, amplifier series of 4 and photomultiplier tube (PMT) voltage of 800 V were used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological characterization of bi-N-CDs and Au NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was applied for observing the morphology of bi-N-CDs ( Fig. 1A). Bi-N-CDs were evenly distributed with interlayer spacing of approximately 0.28 nm, indicating the (020) plane of graphite [34]. The diameter of bi-N-CDs ranged from 1.7 to 3.3 nm and had average size of 2.3 nm (Fig. 1B). Fig. 1C illustrates the morphology of Au NPs, which were uniform in size with diameters ranging from 16.5 to 19.5 nm (Fig. 1D).

The elemental composition of bi-N-CDs was confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) ( Fig. 2A), showing characteristic peaks for C1s (284.80 eV), O1s (532.25 eV), and N1s (401.65 eV). The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2B) reveals three characteristic peaks at binding energies of 284.78 eV (C–C/CC), 286.08 eV (CN), and 288.08 eV (CO) [35]. The high-resolution N1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2C) reveals three characteristic peaks at binding energies of 399.28 eV (pyrrolic N), 401.38 eV (graphitic N), and 406.28 eV (N-H) [36]. The high-resolution O1s spectrum is displayed by a peak at 531.88 eV, consistent with CO bonding (Fig. 2D) [37].

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was performed to characterize CDs and bi-N-CDs ( Fig. 3 A). Both CDs (black line) and bi-N-CDs (red line) had absorption bands at 3355 cm−1 and 2916 cm−1 caused by O-H and C-H stretching vibration [38], respectively. The stripes of bi-N-CDs at 1243 cm−1, 1550 cm−1 and 3049 cm−1 corresponded to C-N stretching vibration, and N-H bending and stretching vibration [39], respectively. Moreover, CDs exhibited a strong CO tensile vibration of carboxyl group at 1713 cm−1, while bi-N-CDs had an obvious CO peak from the amide group at 1670 cm−1, indicating that the introduction of ammonia water changed the carboxyl groups on CDs to amide groups, thus passivating the surface of bi-N-CDs [13].

Ultraviolet-visible (UV−vis) absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of bi-N-CDs aqueous solution were explored. UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 3B) displayed a distinct peak at 373 nm for the n-π* transition of CC bond [40]. The maximum emission wavelength of PL was 460 nm at 378 nm of excitation wavelength (Fig. 3B).

The three-dimensional (3D) ECL spectrum was detected at bi-N-CDs/GCE with 10 mM S2O8 2- in 0.10 M PBS. The maximum ECL emission at 580 nm was observed in the 3D ECL spectrum of bi-N-CDs (Fig. 3 C), which was red-shifted by 120 nm as compared to PL emission of bi-N-CDs (Fig. 3D). The reasons for such a large wavelength difference were fully discussed through electrochemical and spectroscopic measurements, and Supplementary Material presents the results (Fig. S1) and discussions. In conclusion, the observed 120 nm red-shift in ECL can be attributed to charge recombination at low energy surface trap states. When electrons were injected into these surface traps within the band gap of bi-N-CDs, they can induce ECL emission with a red-shift relative to PL emission [41]. Furthermore, it was confirmed that the cathode ECL emission at 580 nm originated from bi-N-CDs/S2O8 2- system rather than O2/S2O8 2- system (681 nm), and Supplementary Material presents the ECL spectral assays (Fig. S2A and S2B) and corresponding discussions.

The appropriate ECL excitation voltage window of bi-N-CDs was explored and Fig. 4A depicts the effect of ECL excitation voltage window on ECL intensity of bi-N-CDs/GCE in 0.10 M PBS containing 10 mM S2O8 2-. When the high potential of the scan was fixed at 0 V and the negative voltage of the scan gradually changed from −1.2 V to −2.0 V, the ECL intensity gradually increased with the increase of the negative potential. At potentials lower than −1.7 V, the ECL intensity was not high. At potentials higher than −1.7 V (such as −1.8 V, −1.9 V and −2.0 V), the ECL intensity would exceed the threshold of MPI-EⅡ ECL analysis instrument (approximately 17600 a.u.). Therefore, the ECL excitation voltage window of 0 ∼ −1.7 V was used for ECL measurement of bi-N-CDs.

Fig. 4B depicts the ECL responses of the differently modified electrodes under the ECL excitation voltage window of 0 ∼ −1.7 V. As seen, the bare GCE had a weak ECL signal (1604 a.u.) with S2O8 2- in 0.10 M PBS (curve a), which was ascribed to the emission from S2O8 2-. Bi-N-CDs/GCE also showed a weak ECL signal (123 a.u.) in 0.10 M PBS without S2O8 2- (curve b), which was ascribed to the emission from bi-N-CDs. The ECL signals of bi-N-CDs/GCE in 0.10 M PBS containing 10 mM S2O8 2- with and without dissolved oxygen were detected and the results are depicted in Fig. 4B curve c and d, respectively. As seen, no significant change in ECL signal intensity was observed upon deoxygenation, suggesting that dissolved oxygen did not participate in the ECL reaction. Obviously, S2O8 2- as a co-reactant effectively promoted the ECL emission from bi-N-CDs.

Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) measurements of bi-N-CDs/GCE were performed in 0.10 M PBS with and without dissolved oxygen (Fig. 4C). In the presence of dissolved oxygen in 0.10 M PBS (curve a), a reduction peak related to dissolved oxygen was observed at −0.47 V, while another reduction peak at −1.47 V was attributed to the reduction of bi-N-CDs. In the absence of dissolved oxygen by purging nitrogen (curve b), the reduction peak related to dissolved oxygen disappeared, while the reduction peak of bi-N-CDs at −1.47 V remained unchanged, demonstrating that dissolved oxygen did not participate in the reduction process of bi-N-CDs. Furthermore, DPV measurements of bi-N-CDs/GCE were performed in 0.10 M PBS containing 10 mM S2O8 2- with and without dissolved oxygen and the results are depicted in Fig. 4D curve a and b, respectively. As seen, the reduction peak of −0.90 V can be detected regardless of whether there was dissolved oxygen or not, which was ascribed to the reduction of S2O8 2- to SO4 •-. The reduction peak of bi-N-CDs shifted to −1.41 V whether there was dissolved oxygen or not. These results clearly demonstrated that S2O8 2- served as a coreactant to effectively promote the reduction of bi-N-CDs. Therefore, the possible ECL mechanism of the bi-N-CDs/S2O8 2- system can be summarized as follows. Initially, under cathode potential scanning, bi-N-CDs and S2O8 2- were separately reduced to bi-N-CDs•- and SO4 •- (Eqs. 1 and 2). Subsequently, bi-N-CDs•- reacted with SO4 •- to produce bi-N-CDs* (Eq. 3). Finally, bi-N-CDs* relaxed to the ground state and produced ECL emission (Eq. 4).
(1) bi-N-CDs + e- → bi-N-CDs•-
(2) S2O8 2- + e- → SO4 2- + SO4 •-
(3) bi-N-CDs•- + SO4 •- → SO4 2- + bi-N-CDs*
(4) bi-N-CDs* →bi-N-CDs + hν

To explore the effect of two N sources (urea and ammonia water) on the ECL performance of bi-N-CDs, control samples including CDs, urea-CDs, NH3-CDs were prepared and their ECL responses were compared in Fig. 4E. In terms of ECL intensity, CDs/GCE exhibited the lowest ECL intensity (curve a), and urea-CDs/GCE (curve b) and NH3-CDs/GCE (curve c) showed higher ECL intensity than CDs/GCE, indicating that the single doping of urea and ammonia water can improve ECL performance of CDs. Bi-N-CDs/GCE exhibited the strongest ECL emission with S2O8 2- in 0.10 M PBS (curve d), which was 5.6, 3.8 and 1.9 times that of CDs/GCE, urea-CDs/GCE, NH3-CDs/GCE, respectively, confirming that the co-doping of urea and ammonia water can significantly improve ECL performance of CDs.

Fig. 4F and its magnified view depict DPV response curves of CDs/GCE, urea-CDs/GCE, NH3-CDs/GCE, and bi-N-CDs/GCE in 0.10 M PBS without S2O8 2-. CDs/GCE exhibited a reduction peak at approximately −1.53 V (curve a). For urea-CDs/GCE (curve b), NH3-CDs/GCE (curve c), and bi-N-CDs/GCE (curve d), the reduction peaks shifted to −1.51 V, −1.48 V, and −1.47 V, respectively. Furthermore, bi-N-CDs/GCE showed a significantly increased peak currents than CDs/GCE, urea-CDs/GCE, and NH3-CDs/GCE, revealing that the co-doping of urea and ammonia water was more conducive to the reduction of CDs, thereby improving the ECL emission of CDs. In fact, the relative ECL efficiencies of urea-CDs, NH3-CDs, and bi-N-CDs were 118 %, 244 %, and 553 %, as compared to CDs/S2O8 2- system. Corresponding calculations are detailed in Supplementary Material
.

In addition, the distinct roles and synergistic effects of urea and ammonia water as nitrogen dopants in improving the ECL performance of bi-N-CDs were fully discussed. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements (Fig. S3) demonstrated that single-source urea doping had a stronger ability to narrow the energy gap than single-source ammonia water doping and their synergistic effect enabled bi-N-CDs to have the smallest energy gap. This narrower energy gap facilitated to enhance ECL efficiency and intensity. Meanwhile, Raman spectroscopy measurements (Fig. S4) demonstrated that single-doped ammonia water showed a stronger defect passivation ability than single-doped urea and their synergistic effect enabled bi-N-CDs to have the highest ratio of the G band intensity to the D band intensity (I G:I D), which facilitated to enhance ECL efficiency and intensity. Details of the results and discussions are provided in Supplementary Material.

Impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and CV were carried out in [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution (5.0 mM) to confirm the assembly of the aptasensor. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the peak currents for oxidation-reduction at Au NPs@bi-N-CDs/GCE (curve b) was significantly lower than that at bare GCE (curve a), which was due to the obstruction of electron transport by bi-N-CDs and chitosan. Subsequently, after incubating the electrode with negatively charged clamp H1, the repulsion between [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- and H1 further reduced the oxidation-reduction peak current (curve c). Finally, incubation of HT on the GCE resulted in a further decreased peak current owing to the hindrance by HT. EIS measurement (Fig. 5B) results were consistent with the CV results, which demonstrated the successful construction of the ECL aptasensor.

The feasibility of the ECL aptasensor was confirmed by ECL measurement (Fig. 5 C). The aptasensor exhibited a strong ECL signal (curve a). When the aptasensor was incubated with the mixture of S4 and Fc-H2, CHA process occurred at the aptasensor and Fc-H2 was assembled on the aptasensor to quench the ECL signal from bi-N-CDs, obtaining a signal-off state (curve b). When the aptasensor was incubated with the mixture containing output S1 and S2, S4 and Fc-H2, S1 and S2 hybridized with S4 to prevent Fc-H2 be introduced on the aptasensor, obtaining a signal-on state (curve c). The target PFOA determined the output of S1 and S2, thereby determining the ECL strength. The above results confirmed the feasibility of ECL aptasensor for detecting PFOA.

The feasibility of the proposed Apt recognition-driven TSDR amplification and the competitive CHA strategy were demonstrated by non-denatured polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) analysis (16 %). Details of the results and discussions can be found in Supplementary Material (Fig. S5) .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response performance of aptasensor to PFOA
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental parameters were optimized. For the molar ratio of double-stranded L0-Apt to triple-stranded L1-S1-S2 (n D:n T), hybridization time of H1, S4 and Fc-H2, volume of H1 and reaction time for TSDR, their optimized values were 1:3 for n D:n T, 2.0 h, 8.0 μL, 2.0 h, respectively. Details of the results and discussions can be found in Supplementary Material (Fig. S6) .

Under the optimized parameters, the prepared ECL aptasensor was used for the quantitative detection of PFOA. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the ECL signal gradually increased with PFOA concentration ranging from 0.10 pM to 1.0 μM. The ECL intensity (I ECL) showed a linear relationship with the logarithm of PFOA concentration, and the linear regression equation was I ECL = 1270.5 lg c + 21050 with a correlation coefficient of 0.9975 (Fig. 6B). The ECL aptasensor exhibited a LOD of 42.7 fM for PFOA and the computational details are presented in Supplementary Material. Compared with existing PFOA detection methods, this aptasensor demonstrated higher detection sensitivity and a wider detection range, as shown in Table S2.

The stability of the aptasensor was explored by detecting the ECL response towards 100 nM, 100 pM, and 100 fM PFOA under continuous scanning for 10 cycles (Fig. 6 C). As seen, the ECL signals of the aptasensor for three different concentrations remained stable, and relative standard deviations (RSDs) were 0.60 %, 1.49 %, and 1.23 %, respectively, indicating a good stability of the ECL aptasensor.

The selectivity of ECL aptasensor was explored using perfluorobutanol (PFB), perfluoro-1-butanesulfonic acid (PFBS), perfluorohexanoic acid (PFHxA), perfluoropentanoic acid (PFPeA) as potential interfering substances. The ECL responses of aptasensor towards 10 μM of interfering substances and 100 nM PFOA are depicted in Fig. 6D. For the blank and other perfluorinated compounds, a low ECL signal was detected. However, the aptasensor exhibited a strong ECL signal for PFOA. Furthermore, the ECL intensity of the mixed group was similar to that of the PFOA group, indicating that other interfering substances did not affect the ECL response of PFOA at aptasensor, demonstrating a good specificity of aptasensor for PFOA.

The reproducibility of the aptasensor was assessed by performing intra-assay and inter-assay tests. In the intra-assay, four distinct GCEs underwent an equivalent modification within a single batch to produce four aptasensors. For the inter-assay, one GCE was modified equivalently across four separate batches, resulting in four aptasensors. As visualized in Fig. 6E, the ECL responses of four aptasensors towards 0.10 nM PFOA did not demonstrate significant differences in intra-assay and inter-assay, with RSDs of 4.21 % and 3.70 %, respectively. This indicates that the developed aptasensor possessed robust repeatability.

The storage stability of the aptasensor against 0.10 μM PFOA was evaluated by maintaining it at 4.0 °C for 7 days with daily ECL measurements. As illustrated in Fig. 6 F, the ECL signal retained over 95 % of its initial response, demonstrating excellent stability of the fabricated aptasensor.

Jialing River water was taken as the actual sample and pre-treated with centrifugation to obtain the extracted sample solution, and ultraperformance liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (UPLC-MS) was taken as the standard method. The actual concentration of PFOA in the extracted sample solution may be lower than the LOD of UPLC-MS/MS and the ECL aptasensor, so it cannot be detected by two methods. Considering the LOD of UPLC-MS/MS for PFOA and the wide linear range of the ECL aptasensor for PFOA (0.10 pM to 1.0 μM), the standard solutions of PFOA at 5.0 nM, 50 nM and 500 nM were selected for the recovery experiment. The spiked sample solutions were analyzed using UPLC-MS/MS and the ECL aptasensor and the results are shown in Table 1. For ECL aptasensor analysis, the recovery rate ranged from 96.6 % to 100.4 %, and RSD was less than 6 %. Using UPLC-MS/MS as a reference, the relative errors (E r) for ECL aptasensor analysis were all within ±3 %. These results demonstrated that the ECL aptasensor exhibited high accuracy in PFOA detection, proving its potential application for PFOA detection in real-world samples.

In this work, urea and ammonia water were co-doped into CDs to prepare bi-N-CDs. As N sources, urea primarily regulated the electronic structure of CDs and ammonia water mainly passivated the CDs surface to inhibit the non-radiative transition in CDs, thus significantly improving ECL emission of bi-N-CDs. The resulting bi-N-CDs exhibited a significantly stronger ECL emission than single-doped CDs by urea or ammonia water. The integration of bi-N-CDs with Apt-driven TSDR established an ultrasensitive ECL system for PFOA detection. The reliability of this aptasensor was validated using UPLC-MS/MS as the standard method. This study explores a new method to improve the ECL performance of CDs while creating a promising ECL system for trace analysis of PFOA and other PECs in the environment.

Tan Ke-Jun: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Shihong Chen: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. Jinwen Zhao: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Hongyan Dong: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (22376171, 21775122), Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing City (CSTB2022NSCQ-MSX0586, cstc2018jcyjAX0693).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2025.138122.

J. Am. Chem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Zheng, Y. Chi, Y. Dong, J. Lin, B. Wang, Electrochemiluminescence of water-soluble carbon nanocrystals released electrochemically from graphite, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 131 (2009) 4564-4565.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Hongyan Dong is a MS candidate in the college of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Southwest University, China. Her main research interests are electrochemiluminescence and biosensors.
Jinwen Zhao is a PhD candidate at the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University. His main research interests are electrochemiluminescence and biosensors.

Shihong Chen is a professor in the college of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Southwest University, China. She received a Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Southwest University, China in 2008. Her main research interests are the biosensors, bio-electroanalysis and nanobiotechnology.

Kejun Tan is a professor in the college of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Southwest University, China. His main research interests are molecular spectroscopic analysis, biochemical drug analysis, separation and analysis of environmental pollutants, synthesis and application of nanomaterials.
